translatedText: Published La Petite Fadette (Little Fadette).
Published Les Maîtres sonneurs (The Master Pipers).
Published her memoirs Histoire de ma vie (Story of My Life).
June 7, 1876
Died.
Career as a Novelist
A French novelist who continued her literary activities after gaining fame with the serial novel Indiana.
Founder of European Women's Literature
Evaluated as a founder of European women's literature alongside Madame de Staël.
Pioneer of the Women's Liberation Movement
Being re-evaluated as a pioneering fighter in the women's liberation movement.
Pinnacle of Epistolary Literature
Her letters (26 volumes) are considered the pinnacle of epistolary literature.
What was George Sand's real name?
Her real name was Amantine Lucile Aurore Dupin.
Who was George Sand?
She was a French novelist also known for her free-spirited romantic affairs.
Where did George Sand's pen name 'Sand' come from?
It is an abbreviation of the surname Sandeau, from her one-time lover Jules Sandeau.
Who were George Sand's famous romantic partners?
The poet Musset and the musician Chopin.
How are George Sand's literary activities categorized?
Her literary career is broadly divided into four periods.
January 19, 1839
Born in Aix-en-Provence.
Entered the Collège Bourbon in Aix and became friends with Émile Zola.
Enrolled in the free municipal school of fine arts in Aix to learn painting.
Enrolled in the law faculty of the University of Aix-en-Provence but devoted himself to drawing practice.
April 1861
Moved to Paris for artistic development but failed to enter the École des Beaux-Arts.
Displayed his paintings at the Salon des Refusés, established by Napoleon III.
January 4, 1872
His son, Paul, was born with Hortense Fiquet.
April 15 - May 15, 1874
Participated in the first group exhibition of the 'Anonymous Society of Artists, Painters, Sculptors, Engravers, etc.'
Exhibited 16 works at the third Impressionist exhibition.
Exhibited Portrait of M. L. A. at the Salon with the help of Antoine Guillemet, marking his first and last Salon exhibit.
April 28, 1886
Married Hortense Fiquet.
October 1886
His father died, and he inherited a fortune, gaining financial stability.
Converted to Catholicism.
November 1895
Ambroise Vollard held Cézanne's first solo exhibition at his Paris gallery, leading to a re-evaluation of his work.
October 25, 1897
His mother died.
Built his own studio in Aix-en-Provence and moved there in 1903.
Exhibited for the first time at the newly established Salon d'Automne.
October 15 - November 15, 1904
An entire room at the Salon d'Automne was dedicated to Cézanne's works.
October 22, 1906
Died of pneumonia at the age of 67.
Two posthumous retrospective exhibitions (at the Bernheim-Jeune gallery and the Salon d'Automne) inspired many artists.
Pioneer of Post-Impressionism
A French Post-Impressionist painter who acted as a bridge between late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.
Creation of a New Pictorial Language
Altered traditional perspective and emphasized the fundamental structure and formal qualities of objects, creating a new pictorial language.
Color Planes and Brushwork Techniques
Characterized by highly characteristic, exploratory brushstrokes, using color planes and small brushstrokes to form complex fields.
Exploration of Geometric Forms
Proposed the pictorial idea of treating nature as simple geometric forms, such as cylinders, spheres, and cones, and placing objects within perspective.
Expression of Depth through Binocular Vision
Provided a new aesthetic experience of depth by exploring binocular vision to express visual perception of the same phenomenon.
Montagne Sainte-Victoire Series
Showcased precise observation of nature and pictorial exploration through a series of paintings featuring Montagne Sainte-Victoire in his hometown of Aix-en-Provence.
Profound Influence on Modern Art
Provided decisive insights and inspiration to early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, including Picasso and Matisse, earning him the title 'the father of us all.'
What kind of painter was Paul Cézanne?
He was a French Post-Impressionist painter who bridged the gap between late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.
How were Cézanne's works received during his lifetime?

His paintings were initially misunderstood and ridiculed by contemporary art critics, but after his first solo exhibition in 1895, they were re-evaluated, and in his later years, he was revered as a master by young artists.
What was Cézanne's influence on modern art?

He influenced the early 20th-century avant-garde art movements by introducing new modes of expression, providing decisive inspiration especially to Cubist and Fauvist painters like Picasso and Matisse.
What are the characteristics of Cézanne's painting techniques?

He altered traditional perspective, emphasized the fundamental structure and formal characteristics of objects, and pursued geometric simplification, treating nature in terms of cylinders, spheres, and cones.
Born in Paris, France.
Studied drawing and painting at the Petite École.
Failed to enter the École des Beaux-Arts and left the Petite École.
His sister Maria died of peritonitis.
Met seamstress Rose Beuret, his lifelong companion.
Submitted his first sculpture, The Man with the Broken Nose, to the Salon, but it was rejected.
Suffered economic hardship due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Traveled to Italy.
Stayed in Italy and returned to France in 1877.
Unveiled The Age of Bronze, causing a social sensation.
Received the commission for The Gates of Hell.
Began his relationship with his student and collaborator, Camille Claudel.
Unveiled The Burghers of Calais.
Unveiled the Monument to Balzac.
Achieved great success as a sculptor and unveiled The Kiss.
Unveiled The Thinker.
Poet Rainer Maria Rilke worked as his secretary.
Unveiled The Walking Man.
Urged by Rainer Maria Rilke to rent the Hôtel Biron, which is now the Musée Rodin.
Unveiled Clemenceau.
Donated his works and collections to the state.
Died.
Progenitor of Modern Sculpture
Known as the progenitor of modern sculpture, he elevated the perception of sculpture to the same level as painting.
Unveiling of The Age of Bronze
Caused a social sensation by unveiling The Age of Bronze in 1876.
Creation of The Gates of Hell
Commissioned in 1880, he created his masterpiece The Gates of Hell, on which he worked for the rest of his life.
Creation of The Burghers of Calais
Created one of his representative works, The Burghers of Calais.
Creation of The Thinker
Created his famous work The Thinker.
Unveiling of the Monument to Balzac
Opened a new path in the field of sculpture by unveiling the Monument to Balzac in 1898.
When was Auguste Rodin born?
Auguste Rodin was born in Paris, France, on November 12, 1840.
What are Auguste Rodin's representative works?
His representative works include The Burghers of Calais, The Gates of Hell, and The Thinker.
How did Auguste Rodin influence the field of sculpture?
He broke the stereotypes of sculpture and elevated its perception to the same level as painting, and is evaluated as the founder of modern sculpture.
When did Auguste Rodin die?
